interactions.summary: Jiang asks what explains Freud's transition from nuanced scientist to myth-maker and gives a material answer: fathers were paying Freud's bills, so a theory blaming fathers threatened his clientele.

He then looks for historical evidence that sexual abuse and taboo transgression were plausible in late nineteenth-century Vienna, connecting Frankism and Sabbatai Zevi to transgressive religious movements and arguing that this context supports the women's testimony.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture using Wikipedia-style source material.

interactions.summary: Jiang introduces Semmelweis as the Vienna warning case: a doctor discovers handwashing prevents childbirth deaths, institutions suppress the finding to avoid blame, and Semmelweis is blacklisted and killed after defying powerful people.

speaker_notes.note: Jiang cites online/Wikipedia material rather than presenting a live second speaker; historical naming is ASR-fragile.

claims.claim: Jiang interprets later Freud as a myth-maker or priestly figure, unlike the early Freud who appeared scientific, nuanced, and balanced.
Freud had a practical incentive to change his story because the fathers of the women he treated were paying his bills and would not accept being blamed for their daughters' trauma.
Jiang presents Frankism as a transgressive religious movement whose taboo-breaking helps make late nineteenth-century Central and Eastern European abuse allegations historically plausible.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical evidence cited in the 2025-06-04 lecture.

claims.claim: Sabbatai Zevi's conversion is used as an example of transgression recoded as true inner faith: outward action matters less than what is in the heart.
Jiang argues that Freud probably knew the women were telling the truth but changed his story to protect his livelihood and avoid punishment by powerful people.
The Semmelweis story functions as Jiang's model of institutional self-protection: evidence that saves lives can be suppressed when it would expose elite responsibility for prior harm.

signature_moments.moment: Freud's theory changes when the person accused by the patient is also the person paying the bill.
signature_moments.source_phrase: who's paying the bills? The father
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This compresses Jiang's power analysis of psychoanalysis into one practical transaction.

signature_moments.moment: The doctors can accept Semmelweis's discovery only if nobody learns what it means: that they had been killing women.
signature_moments.source_phrase: their reputation would be in tatters
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The analogy makes institutional denial the real danger facing both Semmelweis and Freud.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A transgressive religious movement Jiang cites as evidence that taboo-breaking cultic milieus existed in Central and Eastern Europe around the relevant historical background.
Jiang's example of a Vienna doctor punished for evidence that threatened institutional reputation, used to explain why Freud might avoid defying powerful people.
A faith model in which taboo-breaking proves courage, empowerment, and inner fidelity to God.

chronology_notes.note: This packet supplies Jiang's causal answer to Freud's reversal: economic dependence on fathers, plausibility of transgressive abuse networks, and the Vienna precedent of punishing doctors who expose elite culpability.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR likely distorts historical names and periods; 'Frankenism' is treated as Frankism and 'Sibetai Zefi' as Sabbatai Zevi from context.
Jiang says the Frankism material is from Wikipedia and invites verification; confidence is medium because the packet pass records his cited argument rather than independently validating the history.
